headline: The Forester Who Built a Forest from Ruins
meta_description: How one Brazilian forester transformed a mined and abandoned forest tract into a world-class research and restoration center over 30 years.
summary_short: In 1977, Renato Moraes de Jesus arrived at a depleted Atlantic Forest property in Espírito Santo. He stayed three decades, creating a model for forest restoration.
body:
In 1977, a young forest engineer moved into a deteriorating house inside a mined-out Atlantic Forest tract in northern Espírito Santo. Opossums rustled through the roof at night. His first priority was obtaining a working telephone.

Renato Moraes de Jesus would spend more than three decades at that place, transforming it into the Vale Natural Reserve—a leading center for Atlantic Forest research and restoration. When he arrived, 200 hectares had already been logged for railway sleepers. Mining executives had forgotten him there, inadvertently giving him the freedom to dream.

He began methodically. First, he stopped fires and hunting. Then he collected specimens, invited researchers, and built infrastructure: laboratories, a herbarium, seed collections, insect collections, a weather station. By 2001, over 2,000 plant species had been documented there.

But Moraes de Jesus understood something crucial: forests outlast people. He documented every project meticulously—methods, data, purpose—so future researchers could continue the work without him. When executives considered cutting the research budget, he offered his own salary instead.

He later helped restore Fazenda Bulcão in Minas Gerais, where he established the technical program that became Instituto Terra. He traveled, wrote papers, and adjusted his methods as results came in. Learning by doing, he called it.

Public accounts credit him with coordinating the planting of millions of native seedlings across multiple restoration projects. Renato Moraes de Jesus died on May 23rd, aged 74, having spent a lifetime proving that damaged forests can recover—if someone stays long enough to understand them.
